entry.title: Document Grounding Joule Custom Agent using SAP AI Core and Microsoft SharePoint - No AWS S3 Needed
entry.content:
The Problem

We wanted to enhance our custom Joule agent in SAP Joule Studio which is being used for ABAP development capable of answering questions about internal development policies, naming convention and broader custom development governance guidelines documents and technical specifications, all stored in Microsoft SharePoint.

The natural starting point was the Joule Studio/Build Control Tower, which provides a UI-driven setup for document grounding. However, we quickly hit a wall: the Control Tower wizard only supports AWS S3 (Object Store) as the document repository. If you want to use SharePoint, the wizard offers no path forward. This leaves enterprise customers who have no S3 infrastructure — and no intention of setting one up — with no obvious route to grounding their Joule agents in SharePoint content.

The alternative is to build the grounding pipeline directly using the SAP AI Core Pipeline API, bypassing the Control Tower entirely. This approach fully supports SharePoint, gives you precise control over which folders are indexed, and produces a pipeline that can be exposed as a Joule Skill and added as a tool to any custom Joule agent in SAP Build.

The documentation for this approach is scattered, inconsistent across versions, and contains several undocumented gotchas. This post documents the exact steps that work.

The Solution

What You Need

  • SAP AI Core Extended plan (grounding is not available on Free or Standard)
  • Microsoft Entra App Registration with Sites.Read.All and Files.Read.All Graph API permissions. Follow  Prepare SharePoint Integration with Joule.
  • A service account with SharePoint read access
Step 1 — Resource Group with the Correct Label

Create a resource group with the grounding label. Many tutorials use the short-form label — it is wrong. Use the full key: ( I had to use full key)

POST /v2/admin/resourceGroups

{
"resourceGroupId": "Document-Grounding",
"labels": [{ "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/document-grounding", "value": "true" }]
}


Step 2 — Create a Generic Secret for SharePoint

The secret stores your Microsoft Graph API credentials. Field names must be exact — user not username, tokenServiceURL not tokenUrl. ( Get the details from sharepoint admin)

All values must be Base64 encoded (on Windows use PowerShell):[Convert]::ToBase64String([System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("value"))).

POST /v2/admin/secrets

AI-Resource-Group: Document-Grounding
{
"name": "sharepoint-secret",
"data": {
"url": "<base64(https://graph.microsoft.com)>",
"authentication": "<base64(OAuth2Password)>",
"user": "<base64(service-account@tenant.net)>",
"password": "<base64(password)>",
"clientId": "<base64(entra-app-client-id)>",
"clientSecret": "<base64(entra-app-client-secret)>",
"tokenServiceURL": "<base64(https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenantId}/oauth2/v2.0/token)>"
},
"labels": [
{ "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/document-grounding", "value": "true" },
{ "key": "ext.ai.sap.com/documentRepositoryType", "value": "MSSharePoint" }
]
}


Both labels are mandatory. Without them, the pipeline cannot locate the secret.

Step 3 — Create the Pipeline

POST /v2/lm/document-grounding/pipelines
AI-Resource-Group: Document-Grounding

{
"type": "MSSharePoint",
"configuration": {
"destination": "sharepoint-secret",
"cronExpression": "0 0 * * *",
"sharePoint": {
"site": {
"name": "YOUR-SITE-NAME",
"includePaths": ["/YourFolder"]
}
}
}
}

Always use includePaths. Without it, the pipeline attempts to index the entire site including hidden system libraries, leading to failures and irrelevant retrieval results.

The destination field is the AI Core Generic Secret name — not a BTP Destination. This is the single most confusing point in the documentation.

Step 4 — Trigger and Monitor

POST /v2/lm/document-grounding/pipelines/trigger

{ "pipelineId": "<your-pipeline-id>", "metadataOnly": false }

Note: the pipeline ID goes in the request body, not the URL. Check status at /pipelines/{id}/status until it shows FINISHED. Only PDF, DOCX, TXT, and MD files are supported — Excel and PowerPoint files cause silent failures.

Step 5 — Query

POST /v2/lm/document-grounding/retrieval/search
AI-Resource-Group: Document-Grounding

{
"query": "your natural language question",
"filters": [{ "id": "<pipeline-id>", "dataRepositoryType": "vector" }],
"maxResults": 5
}

The dataRepositoryType: "vector" field is required — omitting it returns a 400 error.

Step 6 — Expose via SAP Build

Wrap the retrieval endpoint in a SAP Build Action (upload an OpenAPI spec), then create a Joule Skill that calls it. Add the skill as a tool to your custom Joule agent.

Custom Action 

  Custom Joule Skill

Joule Skill is presented as a tool to the Custom Joule agent. 

Conclusion and Advantages

Building document grounding directly against SharePoint using the AI Core Pipeline API gives you a clean, enterprise-ready RAG pipeline with several advantages:

No S3 required — your documents live where they already are. No migration, no sync jobs, no additional object store costs.

Folder-scoped indexing -  includePaths lets you ground your agent against a specific document set rather than an entire site, giving you precise control over what the LLM can retrieve.

Automatic re-indexing — the cron expression keeps your vector store current without any manual intervention.

Native SAP integration — the retrieval endpoint plugs directly into SAP Build Actions and Joule Studio, making it a first-class tool for custom Joule agents.

Separation from Joule Studio Control Tower — the Control Tower UI creates its own pipelines and does not support folder scoping. The direct API approach gives you full control.

The key takeaways:  Use the Extended AI Core plan, get the Generic Secret field names exactly right, always scope to a folder with includePaths, and remember the pipeline trigger takes its ID in the body. Everything else follows the documentation

AI Core API References : https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core/generative-ai/grounding-035c455a5a424697b60f4a24b6d791fe 

Note: Generative AI used to format the text meaningfully.

entry.title: Dependencies Link Between item with item
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I'm working on SAP S/4HANA 2023 and trying to read Portfolio Item-to-Item dependencies at database level. These are the dependencies shown on the Relationships → Dependencies tab of a Portfolio Item.

I found that the dependency data is stored in table /RPM/RELATION_D (Relationship Type = RII). For one dependency record I see:

  • GUID2 → matches a GUID in /RPM/ITEM_D perfectly → resolves correctly to the dependent item (e.g. ART-CMMS-261195).
  • GUID1 → should point to the source / determining item, but it does NOT match any GUID in /RPM/ITEM_D.

The strange part: GUID1 is almost identical to the source item's GUID in /RPM/ITEM_D — they differ by only one nibble, always at the 4th hex character from the end.

Example:

/RPM/RELATION_D GUID1 = 020ED47FA8811FD198E0B0E4EBE68C9B
/RPM/ITEM_D GUID = 020ED47FA8811FD198E0B0E4EBE6CC9B (the real source item)
differs here (8 vs C)

This difference is consistent across all records I checked — it's clearly not a typo, but some systematic storage/format difference.

My questions:

  1. Why is GUID1 in /RPM/RELATION_D stored in a different format from the GUID field in /RPM/ITEM_D? Is it a different GUID representation (e.g. RAW16 vs CHAR32, or a byte/nibble-order difference)?
  2. What is the correct way to convert or match GUID1 back to /RPM/ITEM_D so I can identify the source/determining item? Is there a standard function module (e.g. GUID_CONVERT or similar) I should use?
  3. Is there a recommended approach to join /RPM/RELATION_D → /RPM/ITEM_D reliably — should I join on GUID at all, or use a different key?

For reference, GUID2 in the same table matches /RPM/ITEM_D with no conversion needed, which makes the GUID1 behavior even more puzzling.

Any guidance would be much appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Driving the Future of Water: SAP Brings Together Water Industry Leaders in Madrid
entry.content:
The water sector is at a transformative crossroad. Utilities and public service organizations worldwide are navigating increasing pressure to modernize operations, strengthen resilience, and deliver smarter, more sustainable services to communities. To meet this moment, SAP is organizing the Executive Value Network (EVN) for Water & Wastewater, next October 21-22 in Madrid as an international knowledge sharing oriented forum for those interested in digital transformation best practices of Water and Wastewater companies.

An Event Built on Strong Partnerships

Hosted by Canal de Isabel II, the Water Company of Madrid  and one of Europe’s most recognized water utilities, it will provide the ideal setting for a gathering of this significance. Farys, the Gent based Belgian water, sewage and municipal services company, will as a strategic advisor, contributing deep sector expertise and a collaborative vision for the future.

Complementing a strong ecosystem of support, Minsait and SEW – Smart Energy and Water join as partners, bringing complementary capabilities in digital transformation, technology integration, and water sector innovation. Together, this network of hosts and partners reflects the very spirit of the event: that progress in the water industry is best achieved through collaboration.

Two Keynotes Not to Be Missed: Canal de Isabel II and ANDA

At the heart of the event program will be two exceptional keynote sessions, each telling a compelling and distinct story about digital transformation in the water sector across very different contexts. Together, they demonstrate that while every utility’s journey is unique, the common purpose is universal: delivering better, more sustainable water services to people and communities.

Representing the host utility, María González Cano from Canal de Isabel II will open with a keynote that offers a mature, large-scale example of how to embed digital capabilities into the very heart of water operations and governance. As one of Europe’s leading metropolitan water utilities, Canal de Isabel II’s keynote sets the tone for the entire event — illustrating what is possible when digital ambition meets operational discipline.

Dagoberto Arévalo, CEO, and Dennis Barrientos, CIO of the Administración Nacional de Acueductos y Alcantarillados (ANDA) from El Salvador will bring a different, but equally powerful perspective to the stage. As a national water authority in an emerging economy, ANDA’s Digital Transformation Journey story is one of determination, pragmatism, and impact — demonstrating that digital transformation is a global imperative, not the exclusive domain of large European utilities by executing their 2025-2030 strategic plan.

 

A Rich Agenda: Three Focused Tracks for Every Stage of the Journey

Beyond the keynotes, the event agenda is structured around three main tracks, designed to help participants move from strategic vision to concrete action, regardless of where they are on their digital journey.

 

Strategic Asset Management: From a Technical Approach to a Citizen-Centric Perspective

Sessions will explore how to build a single source of truth for assets by bringing together technical, financial, operational, and service data; how to apply predictive analytics and risk-based planning to prioritize investments where they matter most for customers and communities; and how to connect asset strategies to resilience, sustainability, and quality of service.

The key shift this track champions is simple but powerful: start from citizens’ needs and work backwards into technology and operations — not the other way around.

This track will include an inspirational session which tackles one of the most fundamental questions in water management: how do you plan and invest in infrastructure so that real people receive reliable, safe, and affordable water services?

Maria Gonzalez (Canal Isabel II) and Inge Opreel (Farys)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Smart Metering

The smart metering track examines how digital measurement and connectivity can transform both operations and customer experience. Topics include architectures for Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) and their integration with SAP back-end systems, turning meter data into actionable insights for leak detection and demand forecasting, enabling new tariff models based on high-frequency consumption data, and managing organizational change as billing, field operations, and customer care become increasingly data-centric.

Sovereign Cloud Strategies

This track addresses one of the most strategic questions facing utilities today: how to embrace cloud innovation while ensuring data protection, compliance, and control. Sessions will cover the concept of sovereign cloud and why it matters for public and critical infrastructure operators, SAP’s approach to supporting data residency, security, and regulatory requirements, and hybrid migration strategies that allow utilities to innovate without disrupting mission-critical operations.

 

Research and Innovation: A Window into What Comes Next

Complementing the three main tracks, the agenda also features a dedicated Research and Innovation stream — designed for those who want to look beyond today’s best practices and anticipate tomorrow’s challenges.

This stream will showcase R&D projects, pilots, and collaborations between utilities, universities, startups, and technology partners. Topics will include AI and machine learning for water operations, digital twins, real-time water quality monitoring, and circular economy initiatives. It is a unique opportunity to discover new ideas and connect with innovators who are already shaping the next generation of water management

 

Networking, Dinner, and Hands-On Experiences

Great events are not only built in the conference room. This program has been designed to foster informal exchange, real-world learning, and lasting connections beyond the formal sessions.

Joint Dinner Sponsored by Minsait: On the evening of the first day, Minsait will host a joint dinner for all participants — a chance to continue the day’s conversations in a more relaxed and convivial atmosphere, explore potential collaborations, and build the kind of relationships that make a real difference back home.

Site Visit on Day Two: Control Center (CPC) and Metrology Laboratory: The second day features an exclusive site visit organized by Canal de Isabel II, offering participants a behind-the-scenes look at how digital strategies translate into real operations, this is connecting what is discussed in the room with what is delivered in the field.

  • Control Center (CPC): See how the Madrid water system is monitored and managed in real time — including how operational data, alarms, and analytics support decision-making and incident response across one of Europe’s largest urban water networks.
  • Metrology Laboratory: Discover how metering devices are tested, calibrated, and certified, and how robust metrology practices underpin reliable billing, smart metering projects, and overall data quality.

 

 

 

 

 

 



Whether you are a utility executive, a digital transformation leader, or a technology innovator, this event offers a unique opportunity to connect with peers, learn from world-class organizations like Canal de Isabel II and ANDA, and explore how SAP’s solutions can help your organization take its next step forward.

We look forward to welcoming you to Madrid.

To register or learn more, visit: https://events.sap.com/evn-water2026/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Custom Sales Order Creation Agent Using Joule Studio
entry.content:
Custom Sales Order Creation Agent Using Joule Studio

Hi Everyone!

In this blog, we will see how we can create a simple custom Joule Agent to create a sales order using Joule Studio. We will also trigger an email notification once the sales order creation is completed successfully.

Pre-requisites

Before starting, we need to ensure that following destinations are configured in SAP BTP subaccount.

Destination to API_SALES_ORDER_SRV API

Destination to Gmail for Email Notifications

This will be used to trigger email notifications once the sales order is created successfully.

 Below is a basic custom agent in Joule Studio consisting of One Joule Agent and One Joule Skill

If we talk about the Joule Agent, I have instructed it to:

- Collect mandatory parameters required to create a Sales Order
- Trigger the sales order creation skill once all required inputs are collected

The overall flow as we can see below :

- Receive the inputs from the user
- Ask the user for confirmation of provided parameters before creating the sales order
- Call the API to create the sales order
- Display the created sales order number to the user
- Trigger an email notification after successful creation

Testing the Agent

Now let’s test the solution.

I will start by asking: “Please help me create a sales order.” As per the instructions, skill sales order create trigged, and requested user to input mandatory parameters.

Let's intentionally skip one mandatory parameter. 

As expected, our agent prompted to provide missing parameter i.e. Material. On the right hand side, we can see below that the agent has successfully extracted the provided parameters.



Now, let’s confirm the details.

Great! The backend API is called successfully, and the sales order 10 is created.

Email Notification Trigger

Once the Sales Order is created successfully, the workflow also triggers an email notification.

Let’s validate the Sales Order in the system too.

We can see that Sales Order number 10 has been created successfully.

This is just a simple example, but the same approach can be used to build more advanced business scenarios.

Stay Tuned

I will be posting more advanced and complex custom Joule agent scenarios soon.

If you have any ideas or use cases that you would like me to build & explore, feel free to share them in the comments section.

Thank you! 😊

entry.title: Re: Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import...
entry.content:
Hi Kazuhiro,

Short version: based on the values shown, this looks like standard behaviour of the payment term, and not a bank‑holiday adjustment – but the exact source of the "1" can only be confirmed by looking at the full configuration of Z120 (see the check at the end).

Why the record you screenshotted may not be the one that calculated this invoice

Z120 is by my opinion a day‑limit (multi‑part) payment term: it can hold more than one record under the same key, and the day of the baseline/document date decides which record is used. The day limit is the day of the month up to which the corresponding terms are valid; for terms that differ before/after a given day you define a multi‑part term under the same key, so there are several records with different terms.

The record you showed has Day Limit 31 and uses Fixed Day 20 / +1 month for the baseline. One thing is certain from the screenshots: that record cannot be the one that produced this invoice, because if it had run, the baseline would be ~the 20th of the following month – not 2026/06/10 – and its term lines are empty, so it would yield Days 1 = 0. Since your invoice shows baseline 2026/06/10 (= posting date) and Days 1 = 1, a different record must have applied. The most likely candidate – to be verified – is an earlier record with a lower day limit (e.g. 15) that uses posting date as baseline and carries a 1‑day term line, but this is an assumption until you check the configuration. See the similar term from my starter demo system:

How the net due date is calculated (the part that is certain)

Regardless of which record applies, the net due date follows one rule: net due date = baseline date + payment period, and the period used is the last one maintained, in this priority: Net Payment Days → else Cash Discount 2 Days → else Cash Discount 1 Days. Net due date = baseline + Cash Discount 1 Days, switching to Cash Discount 2 Days only when that is greater than 0; the standard routine confirms this with worked examples (baseline + Cash Discount 1 Days = 14 → due date 14 days later; only a maintained second period overrides it).

Applied to your invoice: only Cash Discount 1 Days = 1 is maintained (Net = 0, CD2 = 0), so net due = 2026/06/10 + 1 = 2026/06/11.

About the Japan / bank‑holiday adjustment

That feature is separate ("Correct Non‑Workday" + a factory calendar on the term) and by definition only acts when a calculated date lands on a Saturday, Sunday or holiday, correcting it to a working day (it controls whether and how a date on a Sat/Sun/holiday is corrected so it falls on a workday,

To confirm in your system (this is the decisive step)

Open Z120 in Maintain Terms of Payment and page through all day‑limit records (Previous Entry). Identify the record selected by the day of your baseline date (the 10th) and check its term line 1 / Net Payment Days and its baseline default. That record is the source of both the baseline and the "Days 1 = 1". If "net due = baseline" is the intended behaviour, either remove the 1‑day line from that record or maintain an explicit net term — note the value cannot be overridden by adding cash‑discount days to the import file while a term key is present. See Sap Note 3042595 

Hope It helps,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Portugal – Support for Original / Duplicate / Triplic...
entry.content:
Under Portuguese legislation (VAT Code and Decree-Law no. 28/2019), invoices must meet specific content, integrity, and traceability requirements; however, there is no legal obligation to print multiple copies nor to label them as “Original”, “Duplicate”, or “Triplicate”.

In transport scenarios (Regime de Bens em Circulação – Decree-Law no. 147/2003), an invoice may also serve as a transport document and must be issued and available at the start of transport. While multiple copies are often used operationally, the law does not mandate a fixed number of copies or specific naming conventions. 

For standard invoicing, additional copies are not required. When a document is reprinted, it should be clearly identifiable as a copy or reprint, ensuring auditability and avoiding confusion with the original.

1. Is this functionality supported in standard?
Layouts defined for Portugal have the information 'Original' for the first printout and 'Cópia' for further printing of the documents

2. Recommended approaches to achieve the requirement
If the business requires multiple labeled copies, please check the possibility to set up the possibility to re-print the documents immediately

3. Is this requirement known by Localization or Output Management teams?
Layouts provided in the Public Cloud are done via Output Management and those have the distinction between the original and the copy.

 

If there is any legal requirement that we failed to consider, please provide us that information for deeper analysis. 

Hope this information is helpful. Best regards, 

Elsa

entry.title: Introducing the Redesigned SAP Supply Chain Management Value Map
entry.content:
SAP Enterprise Support value maps connect you to structured, product-level groups with curated learning content, tailored recommendations, and expert guidance — included with every SAP cloud subscription. Whether you are building skills, improving operations, or planning for innovation, value maps give you the right foundation to succeed. 

One of those value maps is dedicated to Supply Chain Management (SCM), and it just got a redesign. As the supply chain portfolio continues to grow, navigating the available guidance, remote services, and enablement content can become overwhelming. That is why we redesigned it to make navigation simpler, faster, and more in line with how you actually work.

Whether you are just starting your SAP supply chain journey or looking to speed up adoption of specific solutions, the value map is built to meet you where you are, organized around the portfolio areas you already know.

 

A Portfolio-Oriented Overview Page

The biggest change is on the Overview page. As soon as you enter the SCM value map, you are welcomed with six Featured Topics, each organized around portfolio area rather than an individual solution within SAP's supply chain offering.  This allows you to quickly find the foundational guidance, services, and enablement content that matters most to you.

The six Featured Topics are: Supply chain planning, Supply chain logistics, Manufacturing, Product lifecycle management, Enterprise asset management, and Business Network, each giving you a direct entry point into the content relevant to that portfolio.

 

SAP Business Network Now in the SCM Value Map 

Another update worth highlighting is the addition of SAP Business Network, now featured alongside the rest of the supply chain portfolio in one place. 

SAP Business Network is a cloud-based network that connects buyers, suppliers, logistics providers, and asset operators. It brings together procurement, logistics, and asset processes in one place, with shared visibility and reliable data. 

To better reflect how customers run their supply chain operations, three SAP Business Network modules are now featured in the SCM value map:

  • Supply Chain
  • Logistics
  • Asset Management

 

Knowledge Base Sub-Page Refreshed

The Knowledge Base sub-page has also been refreshed. It is still your go-to starting point for foundational guidance from SAP Enterprise Support, covering design, planning, manufacturing, delivery, operations, and connected business networks. Its purpose remains to help you establish a solid foundation, safeguard solution quality during implementation and operation, and support the adoption of new innovations.

What is new is the consolidation into a single Continuous Quality Checks topic across SCM solutions, and the introduction of AI for Supply Chain.

Continuous Quality Checks for Supply Chain

The Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services are organized by SCM portfolio. CQCs are remote services offered by SAP Enterprise Support at no additional cost that analyze your project, system, or specific situations. They help identify and reduce technical and operational risks, validate solution usage and configuration in line with SAP best practices, and highlight opportunities for optimization and continuous improvement. Each CQC engagement results in a structured report that includes an executive summary, key findings, and actionable recommendations.

AI for Supply Chain

A dedicated topic covering how SAP Business AI is embedded across the supply chain portfolio. From intelligent forecasting and automated logistics execution to predictive maintenance, it is a great place to start if you want to understand and adopt AI capabilities across your supply chain solutions.

 

What This Means for You

The SCM value map redesign is more than a visual update. It is a structural alignment with the SAP supply chain portfolio, making it easier to access the foundational content for solutions under each portfolio. Whatever stage you are at in your journey, the value map now gives you a clearer starting point and a faster path to the content and services you need.

Ready to explore? Check out the Supply Chain Management value map.

 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.

 

About the author: 
Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, she has taken on diverse roles at SAP that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

 💡  Have questions, comments or feedback? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @MabelleRM.

entry.title: Re: gCTS: parallel development on the same server
entry.content:
Hi,
also with gCTS, there can only be one active version of an ABAP object in a system. This means that only one branch of a repository can be active and only one developer can work on the same object at a time. 
You can have different versions of an object in different branches, switch the branches in one system and later on merge the branches. But this means that the different developers have to work sequentially, not at the same time.

Kind regards
karin

entry.title: Re: AI: Building an AI Chat Application on SAP BTP with OpenAI, MCP Server, and SAPUI5 Frontend
entry.content:
Hi @saurit27 ,

You may use SAP BAS for the development.

I prefer the VS Code.

So, step by step:

- Create respective files: 

- manifest.yml
- mta.yml
- package.json
- server.js
- xs-security.json

the source codes are attached above. OpenAI key you have to generate at OpenAI.com, API AI Platfrom

3. Deploy the application

cf login -> login to the Cloud Foundry

cf push mcp-server -- random route ->  Deployment

cf create-service xsuaa application mcp-xsuaa -c xs-security.json -> XSUAA creation

cf bind-service mcp-server XSUAA -> Service binding

cf restage mcp-server -> Application(server) restart

You should see that your application is running if everything is fine.

In you SAP BTP in the section Applications, you should see the running application.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Business Partner Address Management
entry.content:
Business Partner Address 
 
In SAP S/4HANA, the Business Partner (BP) acts as a centralized master data object. It serves as a single point of entry for managing all master data related to Customers, Vendors, and Suppliers. 

Unlike the older SAP ECC system, which requires separate transactions (XD01, VD01, XK01, etc.) to maintain separate customer and vendor master data, the Business Partner model in S/4HANA consolidates these entries. This helps avoid data redundancy and streamlines transaction processing. 

When we look into Business Partner Addresses, we are specifically dealing with the geographic location and communication contact details maintained for that particular BP. 

Major Types of Business Partners 

There are three primary categories of Business Partners in SAP : 

  • Person: Refers to an individual (e.g., a specific employee, private customer, or contact person). 
  • Organization: Refers to a legal entity or company (e.g., a corporate client, vendor, or subsidiary). 
  • Group: Refers to a collection of persons or organizations (e.g., a household or a buying consortium). 

 When it comes to Business Partner Address handling, the behavior is more or less similar across all BP types. 

However, there is a minor difference between Person BP and Organization BP. 
 
When creating an Organization BP, an address number is mandatory and automatically assigned to the data record.  

However, for a Person BP, a physical address number is not strictly mandatory at the time of creation. Instead, a unique person number is generated in the BUT000 table, which points to person-centric address details stored in the ADRP table. 

 
In the below Screenshot, have created a Person BP without Address. 

If you see the BP entry in table BUT000 (General Business Partner Data), you can observe the generated Person Number. 

Maintaining a Business Partner Address 

When creating or updating a Business Partner address, the following location details are generally maintained: 

  • Street 
  • House Number 
  • Postal Code 
  • City 
  • Country 
  • Region 

 
Mandatory Field Validation 

Country is a strictly mandatory field. 

If the Country field is not maintained, SAP throws the following standard validation error:  
"Address is incomplete; please enter Country/Region". 
 

Once the address is successfully maintained and saved, it becomes visible under the Address Overview tab of the Business Partner master data. 

Address Validity and the Standard Address 

 By default, when a new address is created, the system sets its validity period from the Date of Creation until the end of technical time (31/12/9999). This first valid address automatically becomes the Standard Address. 

 

Important Rule 

Every active Business Partner must contain at least one valid Standard Address. 

If the validity of the Standard Address expires and no new valid address exists, the system throws an error during transaction processing. 
The Standard Address is used as the default address in standard SAP transactions. 
 

The created address entries can also be seen in backend table BUT020. 

Communication Types in Business Partner 

Within a Business Partner record, communication details (such as Mobile Number, Telephone, Email Address, and Fax) are split into two categories: 

  1. Independent Communication 
  1. Dependent Communication 

 

Independent Communication 

These contact details are linked directly to the Business Partner as an entity, independent of any physical address. 

Example: Your personal mobile number or personal email ID that travels with you regardless of which office location you sit in. 

These communication details remain valid even if the physical address changes. 
 

The Address Number for Independent Communication is stored in table BUT000 under field ADDRCOMM. 

Since it is directly linked to the Business Partner, the communication reference is available in the General Business Partner Data table itself. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the ADDRCOMM value from BUT000. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6. 

 

Dependent Communication 

These contact details are explicitly linked to a specific physical address. 
 
Example: Imagine you are an employee at XYZ Company. You have a company-specific email ID linked to the corporate headquarters address. If you change companies or if that corporate office address changes, your company-specific email address becomes invalid, whereas your personal/independent communication details remain active. 
If the physical address changes or becomes invalid, the communication details associated with that address may also become invalid. 
 

The Address Number for Dependent Communication is stored in table BUT020. 

Since it is linked to a specific address, we need to fetch the Address Number from the BP Address table. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the Address Number from BUT020. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6.

 
Multiple Addresses & Address Usages 

A Single Business Partner can hold multiple addresses concurrently (e.g., a person can have a Residential Address, a Permanent Address, and a Work/Company Address). 

To manage how these addresses behave, SAP uses Address Usages. 

Examples: 

  • Billing Address 
  • Ship-To Address 
  • Standard Address 

A single address can simultaneously serve multiple purposes. 

Creating Multiple Addresses 

To create multiple addresses: 

  • Navigate to the Address Overview tab. 
  • Click on the Create button. 

Example, if we want to create a Billing Address: 

  • Double-click on Billing Address under Address Usages. 
  • Maintain the required address details. 

Note: SAP allows multiple addresses for the same usage type through SPRO configuration. 
 
SPRO Path 

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner → Basic Settings → Address Determination → Address Types 

Under this configuration, SAP provides a checkbox called Multiple Usages

If this checkbox is enabled: 

Multiple physical addresses can be assigned to the same Address Usage type.

 

All assigned Address Usages are stored in backend table BUT021_FS.

Address Programmatic Interface (BAPIs) 

When manipulating Business Partner addresses via custom ABAP code or interface developments, you should avoid direct database updates. Instead, use the following standard BAPIs: 

  • BUPA_ADDRESS_ADD — To append a new address to an existing BP. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_CHANGE — To modify existing address lines or fields. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_REMOVE — To delete or clear an address from a BP record. Etc... 

Conclusion 

Understanding SAP Business Partner Address Management and its backend table relationships is very important for both technical and functional consultants working in SAP S/4HANA environments. 

Having proper knowledge of: 

  • Address Validity 
  • Standard Address 
  • Independent and Dependent Communication 
  • Multiple Address Usage 
  • Backend Table Relationships 
  • Standard BP Address BAPIs 

helps significantly during: 

  • Support activities 
  • Incident analysis 
  • Debugging 
  • Interface development 
  • Custom enhancements 

I hope this blog provides a useful understanding of SAP Business Partner Address concepts and helps consultants working on real-time SAP BP scenarios.

entry.title: Understanding Security in SAP Joule’s Runtime
entry.content:
Every time you ask Joule a question, your request passes through multiple layers of security, each one independently verifying, isolating, and constraining what happens. Here's what that journey looks like from the inside

Every Joule request flow through two core runtime layers: the Orchestration Runtime, where scenarios are resolved and reasoning happens, and the Execution Runtime, where Joule Functions run. The sections below trace your request through these layers, and the security controls that travel with it.

1. At the Front Door - Proving Who You Are

The moment you interact with Joule; your identity is verified through OAuth2 via SAP Identity Authentication Service. A signed token is issued, and this isn't a one-time check. Every single service in Joule's pipeline independently re-validates that token.

Layer: Runtime Entry

2. Everywhere - Controls That Follow Your Request Across All Layers

Some security controls aren't tied to a single layer; they're enforced uniformly at every boundary your request crosses.

Your identity is re-verified at every hop. All inter-service communication uses mutual TLS (mTLS), both sides of every connection prove their identity. There is no "trusted internal zone." Whether your request is at the runtime entry, orchestration, or execution layer, the service handling it independently validates your authentication token and the calling service's certificate. This creates a zero-trust internal mesh.

Your data is isolated from every other customer's. Each tenant's data lives in its own isolated database schema, Joule cannot query across tenant boundaries. Conversation identifiers are cryptographically derived, making them impossible to guess or enumerate. Your conversations, context, and business data are unreachable by any other customer on the platform.

Applies to: All layers

3. Routing Your Request - Only What You're Allowed to Invoke

Once authenticated, your request reaches Joule's orchestration layer. Before any AI processing begins, your available scenarios are filtered.

Joule supports role-based access to AI scenarios, capability developers define visibility conditions that restrict which scenarios are available to which users. These conditions can be based on navigation authorizations or identity attributes from the user's authentication token. A finance analyst and a warehouse manager see different Joule scenarios, enforced server-side, not by hiding UI elements.

Layer: Orchestration Runtime

4. Safer Conversations - Filtering Harmful Content

Joule's reasoning runs through large language models accessed via SAP's centralised AI orchestration platform. That platform brings a content-safety layer with it, and Joule turns it on by default. 

The filter screens AI inputs and outputs for harmful content and operates on both sides of the model:

- Before your message reaches the LLM, the input is checked.

- Before any model-generated text returns to you, the output is checked.

The platform attaches the filter automatically when an AI call is made. Capability developers don't have to remember to switch it on; safety is the default path.

By consolidating on a centrally managed safety service rather than per-team guardrails, Joule benefits from continuous improvements to the underlying classifiers without every capability needing to change its own code.

Layer: Orchestration Runtime

5. Taking Action - The AI Can't Run Wild

When Joule resolves your request into a backend action such as querying an SAP system, updating a record, running a calculation, etc. that action executes inside a hardened sandbox:

- Expression sandbox: only pre-approved operations can execute. Arbitrary code is structurally impossible; the sandbox uses a strict allowlist of permitted types and methods.

- Template engine: scripting is limited to template logic, not general-purpose computation. The engine evaluates structure, never executes code.

- Resource governors: execution timeouts, response size caps, and iteration limits prevent any single action from consuming unbounded resources.

Even in an adversarial scenario, the execution environment constrains what can physically happen, code execution and resource exhaustion are architecturally prevented.

Layer: Execution Runtime

6. Reaching Backend Systems - Only Where You've Approved, Only What You Can See

Joule doesn't have open network access. All outbound connectivity is routed through a fixed, admin-configured allowlist of approved hosts. Combined with IP-range blocking, this means:

- Joule can only communicate with systems your organisation has explicitly permitted

- No AI-generated URL or parameter can redirect connectivity to an unapproved host

When Joule does reach a backend system, it acts “as you”, not as a privileged service account. 

Backend calls use principal propagation, meaning your existing authorization rules are enforced by the backend itself. If you can't access a purchase order in SAP directly, you can't access it through Joule either.

Your administrators control the network boundary. Your backend controls the data boundary. Joule inherits both.

Layer: Execution Runtime

7. The Response - Secure All the Way Back

The response travels back through the same authenticated, encrypted service mesh. Every hop is mTLS-protected, every service re-validates the request context, and tenant isolation ensures your response data never intermingles with another customer's pipeline.

Security by Architecture, Not by Afterthought

Joule wasn't secured after the fact - security is built into how requests flow through the system. Identity is verified at every boundary. Data is isolated at the infrastructure level. Code execution is sandboxed by design. Network access is bounded by admin policy.

The result: an enterprise AI assistant where security isn't a layer on top, it's the architecture itself.

entry.title: Using Domain Fixed Values as Value Help in SAP RAP Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP RAP Fiori applications, value help improves the user experience by providing predefined selectable values for filter and input fields.

One simple and efficient approach is to use ABAP Domain Fixed Values as value help instead of creating separate CDS value help entities.

In this blog, we will see how to implement value help in Fiori Elements using Domain Fixed Values in SAP RAP.

Business Requirement

In a SAP RAP Fiori Elements application, users should be able to select predefined status values through Value Help instead of entering values manually.

The Status field should display fixed values maintained in an ABAP Domain, such as:

  • 100 – Not Prepared
  • 101 – In Preparation
  • 102 – Completed

    When the user opens the value help in the Fiori application, the system should automatically display these fixed domain values along with their descriptions.

    Step 1:  Create a Domain with Fixed Values

    Before building the Value Help CDS View, we first need to create an ABAP Domain that contains the fixed status values.

    The domain will act as the source for the Value Help values displayed in the Fiori application.

     
    Step 2: Build the Value Help CDS View
    SAP provides two standard tables that expose domain fixed values:
       1. DD07L  - Contains the fixed values of the domains.
       2. DD07T - Language-dependent description texts.
    We join these two views and pass our domain name as a parameter. The join on `$session.system_language` is important — without it, you'd get one row per language in the system, causing duplicates in the dropdown.
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Status'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_BP_STATUS_VH 
as select from dd07l as values
    inner join   dd07t as texts on  values.domname    = texts.domname
                                and values.domvalue_l = texts.domvalue_l
                                and texts.ddlanguage  = $session.system_language
{
      @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
  key values.domvalue_l as StatusCode,
      texts.ddtext      as StatusText
}
where values.domname = 'ZDO_VD_STATUS'
and texts.domname = 'ZDO_VD_STATUS'.

 

Step 3: Attach the Value Help to the Main CDS View

Now go to the Projection View (Consumption CDS View) where the Status field is defined.

Use the annotation @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition to connect the field with the Value Help CDS View.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@Search.searchable: true
@ObjectModel.semanticKey: [ 'ExternalId' ]
define root view entity ZC_BP_REQHDR
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on ZI_BP_REQHDR
{

  key     RequestUuid,

          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          ExternalId,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'RequesterName' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'I_BusinessPartnerVH',
                                                         element: 'BusinessPartner' } }]
          RequesterId,
          _Requester.BusinessPartnerName as RequesterName,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_STATUS_EDIT_VH',
                                                          element: 'StatusCode' } }]
          Status,
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_STATUS_VH',
                                                         element: 'StatusCode' } }]
          //          Status                         as StatusFilter,
          _status.StatusText             as StatusText,
          StatusCriticality,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'PriorityText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_PRIORITY_VH',
                                                         element: 'PriorityCode' } }]
          @Consumption.filter.selectionType: #SINGLE
          Priority,
          _priority.PriorityText         as PriorityText,
          DeadlineDate,
          CancelReason,

          @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
          @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_BP_CANCEL_REQUEST'
  virtual CancelReasonHidden : abap_boolean,

          LastChangedAt,

          _items : redirected to composition child ZC_BP_REQITEM,
          _Requester
}

 

Value Help Configuration in RAP

The below annotations are used to enable Value Help for the Status field in SAP RAP Fiori Elements.

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition

This annotation connects the field with a CDS Value Help entity.

ZI_BP_STATUS_VH

This CDS View acts as the Value Help provider and supplies the fixed domain values.

element: 'StatusCode'

Specifies which field from the Value Help entity should be returned to the main field.

@ObjectModel.text.element

This annotation helps display the description text together with the code value in the Fiori UI.

With this configuration, users can select predefined status values directly from the Fiori Value Help dialog instead of entering values manually.

 

Step 4: Expose the CDS View in Service Definition

After creating the Value Help CDS View and Projection View, the next step is to expose the CDS entity through a Service Definition.

@EndUserText.label: 'Request Services'
define service ZUI_BP_REQUEST {
  expose ZC_BP_REQHDR;
  expose ZC_BP_REQITEM;
}

 

Output

After activating the CDS Views, Service Definition, and Service Binding, the Status field in the Fiori Elements application will display a Value Help popup containing the fixed values maintained in the ABAP Domain.

 

Conclusion

In this blog, we saw how to implement Value Help using ABAP Domain Fixed Values in a SAP RAP Fiori Elements application without creating custom database tables or complex Value Help logic.

By using the standard tables DD07L and DD07T, we were able to:

  • Expose domain fixed values directly in the Fiori UI
  • Display meaningful descriptions alongside technical keys
  • Improve the user experience with predefined selections
  • Avoid unnecessary custom Value Help entities
  • Build a lightweight and reusable solution

This approach is simple, efficient, and easy to maintain. Any changes made to the domain fixed values are automatically reflected in the Fiori application without additional development effort.

I hope this blog helps you implement Value Help efficiently in your SAP RAP applications.

entry.title: Re: WBS check clsd balance 0 due to revenue and cost
entry.content: There is no standard customizing or function switch to enforce this. The only way to achieve your requirement is via an enhancement — BAdI AT_SAVE (or equivalent user exit in on-premise). That is the correct solution path if you want to block CLSD status until settlement has been performed.

entry.title: Request for BADI to Enhance Inbound Delivery Validation
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We would like to implement an enhancement in the S/4HANA inbound delivery process and would appreciate your guidance on the appropriate BADI that can be used to make changes to inbound deliveries.

The business requirement is to prevent duplicate vendor invoice numbers during inbound delivery creation. Specifically, if a user enters a vendor invoice number that has already been maintained for another inbound delivery, the system should validate the entry and trigger an error message, preventing the creation or saving of the duplicate record.

Could you please advise on the suitable BADI or enhancement framework that can be used to implement this validation?

Thank you in advance for your support.

BR,

Abhishek

entry.title: Re: customer stock B active
entry.content: ECC EHP8 does not have a native “customer stock” function. The closest standard options are sales order stock (special stock E) or returns/repair order processes. If you want the exact “customer stock” feature you have seen in S/4HANA Cloud, you would need either to adapt sales order stock or build a small enhancement.

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence & SAP EPPM
entry.content:
AI is fundamentally reshaping SAP Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management (EPPM) by enabling intelligent automation, predictive analytics, and data-driven decision-making across the full project lifecycle. By integrating capabilities such as machine learning, natural language processing, and generative AI, SAP EPPM is evolving from a traditional project tracking platform into a proactive, intelligent management system — helping organizations align project investments with strategic objectives, optimize resources, and mitigate risks before they escalate.

Key AI Capabilities in SAP EPPM

 

  • Intelligent Project Navigation (NLP): The Joule Copilot's Navigational Project Assistant uses natural language processing to allow users to find and open applications displaying project data, directly access projects, Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements, or networks in the Project Builder — reducing navigation effort and improving user satisfaction. 
  • AI-Driven Resource Skill Matching: The Advanced Skill Match feature in SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM), powered by SAP Business AI, automates skill matching and similarity analysis — identifying candidates with exact or similar competencies and ranking them by relevance and availability. 
  • Predictive Risk & Early Warning Detection: Agentic AI can detect early warning signals across project variables, helping teams mitigate risk before issues escalate. 
  • Automated Portfolio Scoring & Prioritization: AI-powered solutions (e.g., built on SAP BTP integrated with large language models) automate the scoring, simulation, and prioritization of project portfolios, replacing subjective manual scoring with data-driven evaluation. 

How AI Enhances Core EPPM Processes

Portfolio Management

AI enables dynamic portfolio prioritization by aligning project plans with strategic objectives. Solutions built on SAP BTP can automate portfolio scoring and simulate project outcomes under different conditions, empowering finance teams and portfolio managers to make informed decisions about which initiatives to pursue, postpone, or cancel. This reduces governance bottlenecks and improves financial transparency.

Risk & Issue Management

A unified data foundation within SAP EPPM enables Agentic AI to dynamically prioritize portfolios and detect early warning signals across project variables — assisting teams to mitigate risk proactively before issues escalate. Additionally, machine learning-based scenarios (such as Risk of Late Payment) predict which customers are most likely to miss payment deadlines, with AI explainability features available to analyze scenario trends. 

Joule Copilot

Intelligent AI assistant embedded in SAP Fiori launchpad for project navigation, planning, and sales order monitoring

SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM)

Cloud-based resource management tool with AI-driven skill matching for ETO and PSA projects

Business Benefits

  1. Enhanced Efficiency: Faster staffing processes through AI-driven skill matching reduce project delays and administrative overhead. 
  2. Improved Decision-Making: Automated portfolio scoring and live simulations enable more informed, data-driven investment decisions — reducing governance bottlenecks. 
  3. Proactive Risk Mitigation: Early warning signal detection across project variables allows teams to address risks before they escalate into issues. 
  4. Improved Resource Utilization: AI aligns workforce capabilities with project needs, ensuring the right people are assigned to the right roles. 
  5. Operational Cost Reduction: Organizations implementing AI-enhanced SAP solutions have experienced considerable reductions in operational costs within the first year of deployment, stemming from automation of labor-intensive processes and reduction of manual errors. 
  6. Better User Experience: Natural language navigation via Joule reduces effort for accessing project data and improves overall user satisfaction.

Future Outlook

  • Agentic AI is emerging as a key direction for SAP EPPM, with intelligent agents capable of dynamically prioritizing project portfolios, aligning plans with strategic objectives, and detecting early warning signals autonomously
  • SAP AI Agents focused on sales, supply chain optimization, pricing strategies, and inventory management are upcoming additions to SAP's AI ecosystem.

"AI is transforming SAP EPPM from a passive project tracking tool into an intelligent, proactive management platform that supports the full project and portfolio lifecycle"
